examples.title: Algorithm: Stack ADT Implementation
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the Abstract Data Type (ADT) concept with Stack operations including push, pop, peek, and isEmpty
examples.code:
ALGORITHM StackADT
INPUT: Stack capacity, operations to perform
OUTPUT: Result of stack operations

// Define the Stack ADT
DATA: A collection of elements with LIFO ordering
OPERATIONS: push(item), pop(), peek(), isEmpty(), isFull()

BEGIN
 // Initialize stack with maximum size
 CREATE stack[MAX_SIZE]
 SET top = -1

 // Operation: isEmpty
 FUNCTION isEmpty()
 IF top == -1 THEN
 RETURN true
 ELSE
 RETURN false
 ENDIF
 END FUNCTION

 // Operation: isFull
 FUNCTION isFull()
 IF top == MAX_SIZE - 1 THEN
 RETURN true
 ELSE
 RETURN false
 ENDIF
 END FUNCTION

 // Operation: push(item)
 FUNCTION push(item)
 IF isFull() THEN
 RETURN "Overflow - Stack is full"
 ELSE
 SET top = top + 1
 SET stack[top] = item
 RETURN "Item pushed successfully"
 ENDIF
 END FUNCTION

 // Operation: pop()
 FUNCTION pop()
 IF isEmpty() THEN
 RETURN "Underflow - Stack is empty"
 ELSE
 SET item = stack[top]
 SET top = top - 1
 RETURN item
 ENDIF
 END FUNCTION

 // Operation: peek()
 FUNCTION peek()
 IF isEmpty() THEN
 RETURN "Stack is empty"
 ELSE
 RETURN stack[top]
 ENDIF
 END FUNCTION

 // Demonstrate Stack ADT operations
 // Example: Push elements 10, 20, 30
 CALL push(10) // top becomes 0, stack[0]=10
 CALL push(20) // top becomes 1, stack[1]=20
 CALL push(30) // top becomes 2, stack[2]=30

 // Peek at top element
 SET topElement = CALL peek() // Returns 30

 // Pop elements (LIFO order)
 SET item1 = CALL pop() // Returns 30, top becomes 1
 SET item2 = CALL pop() // Returns 20, top becomes 0
 SET item3 = CALL pop() // Returns 10, top becomes -1

 // Check if empty
 SET emptyCheck = CALL isEmpty() // Returns true

 RETURN "Stack ADT demonstration complete"
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the Abstract Data Type (ADT) concept for a Stack. An ADT defines WHAT operations are available without specifying HOW they are implemented. The Stack follows LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle. Key operations: push adds to top, pop removes from top, peek views top element without removal, isEmpty/isFull check status. The ADT does not specify whether implementation uses array or linked list - that is an implementation detail.

examples.title: Python: Data Structure Comparison and Operations
examples.description: Python implementation demonstrating different data structures (array/list, stack, queue) and their time complexities
examples.code:
# Introduction to Data Structures - Python Implementation
# Demonstrates Arrays (Lists), Stacks, and Queues in Python

# =============================================================================
# 1. ARRAY OPERATIONS (Using Python List)
# =============================================================================

class ArrayOperations:
 """Demonstrates array/list operations and time complexity"""
 
 def __init__(self):
 self.array = []
 
 def insert_at_end(self, item):
 """O(1) - Insert at the end of array"""
 self.array.append(item)
 return self.array
 
 def insert_at_position(self, position, item):
 """O(n) - Insert at specific position"""
 if 0 <= position <= len(self.array):
 self.array.insert(position, item)
 return self.array
 return "Invalid position"
 
 def access_by_index(self, index):
 """O(1) - Direct access by index"""
 if 0 <= index < len(self.array):
 return self.array[index]
 return "Index out of bounds"
 
 def linear_search(self, target):
 """O(n) - Search element"""
 for i, val in enumerate(self.array):
 if val == target:
 return f"Found at index {i}"
 return "Not found"
 
 def delete_at_position(self, position):
 """O(n) - Delete at specific position"""
 if 0 <= position < len(self.array):
 return self.array.pop(position)
 return "Invalid position"


# =============================================================================
# 2. STACK IMPLEMENTATION (LIFO - Last In, First Out)
# =============================================================================

class Stack:
 """Stack data structure using Python list"""
 
 def __init__(self, max_size=None):
 self.items = []
 self.max_size = max_size
 
 def is_empty(self):
 """O(1) - Check if stack is empty"""
 return len(self.items) == 0
 
 def is_full(self):
 """O(1) - Check if stack is full"""
 if self.max_size is None:
 return False
 return len(self.items) == self.max_size
 
 def push(self, item):
 """O(1) - Add item to top of stack"""
 if self.is_full():
 return "Overflow - Stack is full"
 self.items.append(item)
 return f"Pushed: {item}"
 
 def pop(self):
 """O(1) - Remove and return top item"""
 if self.is_empty():
 return "Underflow - Stack is empty"
 return self.items.pop()
 
 def peek(self):
 """O(1) - View top item without removing"""
 if self.is_empty():
 return "Stack is empty"
 return self.items[-1]
 
 def display(self):
 """Display stack contents"""
 return self.items


# =============================================================================
# 3. QUEUE IMPLEMENTATION (FIFO - First In, First Out)
# =============================================================================

class Queue:
 """Queue data structure using Python list"""
 
 def __init__(self):
 self.items = []
 
 def is_empty(self):
 """O(1) - Check if queue is empty"""
 return len(self.items) == 0
 
 def enqueue(self, item):
 """O(1) - Add item to rear of queue"""
 self.items.append(item)
 return f"Enqueued: {item}"
 
 def dequeue(self):
 """O(1) - Remove and return front item"""
 if self.is_empty():
 return "Queue is empty"
 return self.items.pop(0)
 
 def front(self):
 """O(1) - View front item without removing"""
 if self.is_empty():
 return "Queue is empty"
 return self.items[0]
 
 def display(self):
 """Display queue contents"""
 return self.items


# =============================================================================
# DEMONSTRATION
# =============================================================================

if __name__ == "__main__":
 print("=" * 60)
 print("DATA STRUCTURES DEMONSTRATION")
 print("=" * 60)
 
 # Array Operations Demo
 print("\n--- ARRAY OPERATIONS ---")
 arr = ArrayOperations()
 arr.insert_at_end(10)
 arr.insert_at_end(20)
 arr.insert_at_end(30)
 print(f"Array after insertions: {arr.array}")
 print(f"Access index 1: {arr.access_by_index(1)}") # O(1)
 print(f"Search 20: {arr.linear_search(20)}") # O(n)
 
 # Stack Operations Demo
 print("\n--- STACK OPERATIONS (LIFO) ---")
 stack = Stack()
 print(stack.push(10))
 print(stack.push(20))
 print(stack.push(30))
 print(f"Stack contents: {stack.display()}")
 print(f"Peek (top): {stack.peek()}")
 print(f"Pop: {stack.pop()}") # Returns 30 (last in)
 print(f"Pop: {stack.pop()}") # Returns 20
 print(f"Stack after pops: {stack.display()}")
 print(f"Is empty: {stack.is_empty()}")
 
 # Queue Operations Demo
 print("\n--- QUEUE OPERATIONS (FIFO) ---")
 queue = Queue()
 print(queue.enqueue(10))
 print(queue.enqueue(20))
 print(queue.enqueue(30))
 print(f"Queue contents: {queue.display()}")
 print(f"Front: {queue.front()}")
 print(f"Dequeue: {queue.dequeue()}") # Returns 10 (first in)
 print(f"Dequeue: {queue.dequeue()}") # Returns 20
 print(f"Queue after dequeues: {queue.display()}")
 
 print("\n" + "=" * 60)
 print("KEY COMPLEXITIES:")
 print(" Array access by index: O(1)")
 print(" Array search: O(n)")
 print(" Stack push/pop: O(1)")
 print(" Queue enqueue/dequeue: O(1)")
 print("=" * 60)
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates three fundamental data structures: Arrays (using Python lists), Stacks (LIFO), and Queues (FIFO). Each class includes common operations with their time complexities annotated. The ArrayOperations class shows O(1) access by index and O(n) search/insert/delete. The Stack class demonstrates push, pop, peek operations all at O(1). The Queue class shows enqueue and dequeue at O(1). This illustrates the key concept from the topic: choosing the right data structure depends on the operations you need to perform most frequently.
examples.timeComplexity: Varies: O(1) for stack push/pop, O(n) for array search
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for all structures where n is number of elements

examples.title: C: Linked List Implementation
examples.description: C implementation of a singly linked list demonstrating dynamic data structure with nodes and pointers
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates a singly linked list, a fundamental non-primitive linear data structure. The key aspects: (1) Each node contains data and a pointer to the next node, (2) Memory is allocated dynamically using malloc, (3) Unlike arrays, linked lists have non-contiguous memory, (4) Insertion at front is O(1) while insertion at end requires traversal O(n), (5) Deletion at front is O(1). This contrasts with arrays where access by index is O(1) but insertion/deletion requires shifting elements. The implementation demonstrates the trade-offs discussed in the topic between different data structures.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for search/insert at end, O(1) for insert at front/delete from front
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of nodes (plus overhead for pointers)

examples.title: Java: Binary Search Tree Implementation
examples.description: Java implementation of a Binary Search Tree (BST) demonstrating non-linear hierarchical data structure
examples.explanation: This Java implementation demonstrates a Binary Search Tree (BST), a non-linear hierarchical data structure. Key concepts from the topic: (1) BST follows the rule: left subtree has smaller values, right subtree has larger values, (2) It enables efficient search, insert, and delete operations at O(log n) average time complexity, (3) The three tree traversals (inorder, preorder, postorder) are fundamental tree algorithms, (4) The tree structure is non-linear - elements are arranged hierarchically with parent-child relationships, (5) Worst case O(n) occurs when the tree becomes unbalanced. This demonstrates the relationship between data structures and algorithms - tree traversal algorithms are specific to tree structures as mentioned in the topic.
